---
title: "Air Quality by City"
description: "Pick a city and see how its air pollutants have changed since the 1970s — annual trends and the month-by-month detail, straight from the NAPS monitoring network"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import os
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
from pipeline.config import get_data_date

# Muted, elegant blue for the city air-quality lines (Tableau-10 blue; not the
# Canada-red reserved for peer comparisons elsewhere on the site).
MUTED = "#4e79a7"

# Pollutant panels (label, units) and their grid positions.
POLLS = [("PM2.5", "µg/m³"), ("O3", "ppb"), ("NO2", "ppb"), ("SO2", "ppb"), ("CO", "ppm")]
POS = [(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 1), (2, 2)]
PREFERRED = ["Toronto", "Montréal", "Vancouver", "Calgary", "Edmonton",
             "Ottawa–Gatineau", "Winnipeg", "Québec City"]
ANN = "data/environment/naps_city_annual.csv"
MON = "data/environment/naps_city_monthly.csv"


def _ordered_cities(df):
    present = set(df["city"])
    head = [c for c in PREFERRED if c in present]
    return head + sorted(present - set(head))


def city_dashboard(df, xcol, *, mode, hovx, source_note, who_line=False):
    """Small-multiples (5 pollutant panels) with a city dropdown that toggles
    which city's series are shown across all panels."""
    cities = _ordered_cities(df)
    fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=3,
                        subplot_titles=[f"{p} ({u})" for p, u in POLLS],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.07, vertical_spacing=0.15)
    trace_city = []
    for city in cities:
        for (p, u), (rr, cc) in zip(POLLS, POS):
            s = df[(df["city"] == city) & (df["pollutant"] == p)].sort_values(xcol)
            fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                x=s[xcol], y=s["value"], mode=mode,
                line=dict(color=MUTED, width=2),
                marker=dict(size=4, color=MUTED) if "markers" in mode else dict(),
                showlegend=False, visible=(city == cities[0]),
                hovertemplate=f"{p} {hovx}: %{{y:.1f}} {u}<extra></extra>"),
                row=rr, col=cc)
            trace_city.append(city)
    if who_line:   # WHO 2021 annual PM2.5 guideline on the PM2.5 panel
        fig.add_hline(y=5, line_dash="dot", line_color="#2ca02c", opacity=0.7, row=1, col=1)
    buttons = [dict(method="update", label=c,
                    args=[{"visible": [tc == c for tc in trace_city]}])
               for c in cities]
    fig.update_xaxes(gridcolor="#e0e0e0")
    fig.update_yaxes(gridcolor="#e0e0e0", rangemode="tozero")
    fig.update_layout(
        height=620, plot_bgcolor="white", margin=dict(t=90, b=80, l=45, r=20),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, active=0, x=0.5, xanchor="center",
                          y=1.20, yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        annotations=list(fig.layout.annotations) + [
            dict(text="Select a city", xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0.5, y=1.27,
                 xanchor="center", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555")),
            dict(text=source_note, xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.13,
                 showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    return fig


def monthly_city_pollutant(m, *, source_note):
    """One large monthly time series with TWO dropdowns — City (restyles the line's
    data) and Pollutant (toggles which of the 5 pollutant traces is shown). The
    menus don't conflict: one changes the DATA, the other changes VISIBILITY. A
    range slider lets you zoom into a period for detail."""
    m = m.copy()
    m["date"] = pd.to_datetime(m["date"])
    cities = _ordered_cities(m)
    dates = sorted(m["date"].unique())
    didx = {d: i for i, d in enumerate(dates)}

    def yfor(city, poll):
        sub = m[(m["city"] == city) & (m["pollutant"] == poll)]
        arr = [None] * len(dates)
        for d, v in zip(sub["date"], sub["value"]):
            arr[didx[d]] = v
        return arr

    c0 = cities[0]
    fig = go.Figure()
    for i, (p, u) in enumerate(POLLS):
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=dates, y=yfor(c0, p), mode="lines", name=p, visible=(i == 0),
            line=dict(color=MUTED, width=1.6), connectgaps=False,
            hovertemplate=f"%{{x|%b %Y}}: %{{y:.1f}} {u}<extra></extra>"))
    city_buttons = [dict(method="update", label=c,
                         args=[{"y": [yfor(c, p) for p, u in POLLS]},
                               {"yaxis.autorange": True}]) for c in cities]
    poll_buttons = [dict(method="update", label=p,
                         args=[{"visible": [pp == p for pp, uu in POLLS]},
                               {"yaxis.title.text": f"{p} ({u})", "yaxis.autorange": True}])
                    for p, u in POLLS]
    fig.update_layout(
        height=480, plot_bgcolor="white", margin=dict(t=100, b=95, l=55, r=20),
        xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.06, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        yaxis=dict(title=f"{POLLS[0][0]} ({POLLS[0][1]})", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", rangemode="tozero"),
        updatemenus=[
            dict(buttons=city_buttons, active=0, x=0.0, xanchor="left", y=1.17, yanchor="top",
                 bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc", borderwidth=1, showactive=True),
            dict(buttons=poll_buttons, active=0, x=0.42, xanchor="left", y=1.17, yanchor="top",
                 bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc", borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        annotations=[
            dict(text="City", xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0.0, y=1.26, xanchor="left",
                 showarrow=False, font=dict(size=11, color="#555")),
            dict(text="Pollutant", xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0.42, y=1.26, xanchor="left",
                 showarrow=False, font=dict(size=11, color="#555")),
            dict(text=source_note, xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
                 showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    return fig
```

Pick a city and see how the air its residents breathe has changed since the 1970s. These
are the actual measurements from Environment and Climate Change Canada's
[National Air Pollution Surveillance (NAPS)](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/air-pollution/monitoring-networks-data/national-air-pollution-program.html)
network — the monitoring stations behind the national indicators on the
[Air Quality](air-quality.qmd) page — averaged across each city's stations. The
broad pattern is improvement: sulphur dioxide and nitrogen dioxide have fallen
sharply in most cities. The exception is fine particulate matter (PM2.5), which has
been pushed up in recent years by wildfire smoke — most visibly in the western
cities in 2023.

## Annual trends

Annual mean concentration for each pollutant — back to **1974** for the gases, the
late 1990s for PM2.5. Use the dropdown to change city. The dotted green line on the
PM2.5 panel is the World Health Organization's 2021 guideline (5 µg/m³).

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(ANN):
    a = pd.read_csv(ANN)
    fig = city_dashboard(
        a, "year", mode="lines+markers", hovx="%{x}", who_line=True,
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, NAPS Annual Summaries (1-hour basis), stations averaged per city | Data as of: {get_data_date(ANN)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("NAPS city data not yet available. Run: python -m pipeline.build_naps_cities")
```

## Month-by-month detail

Monthly resolution reveals the seasonal rhythm — summer ozone, winter particulates —
and the sharp spikes when wildfire smoke arrives. Pick a **city** and a **pollutant**
to see one series at full size, and drag the slider beneath the chart to zoom into a
period. Try Edmonton or Calgary with PM2.5 around 2023 for a record smoke season.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(MON):
    m = pd.read_csv(MON)
    fig = monthly_city_pollutant(
        m,
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, NAPS Annual Summaries (monthly means, 1-hour basis), stations averaged per city | Data as of: {get_data_date(MON)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("NAPS monthly data not yet available. Run: python -m pipeline.build_naps_cities")
```

## Download Data

- [Air quality by city — annual (CSV)](../data/environment/naps_city_annual.csv) — ECCC NAPS Annual Summaries
- [Air quality by city — monthly (CSV)](../data/environment/naps_city_monthly.csv) — ECCC NAPS Annual Summaries

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## About these data

**Source & method.** Values are 1-hour-basis concentrations from the NAPS network
(per-station). For each city we average all of its stations, so a city with several
monitors is smoothed across them. Units are µg/m³ for PM2.5 and ppb for the gases
(ppm for CO).

**Coverage & how far back.** About 30 of Canada's larger cities are included. The
gases (NO₂/SO₂/O₃/CO) reach back to **1974** and PM2.5 to the **late 1990s**. From
2005 on the values are NAPS's pre-computed *Annual Summaries* (validated per-station
means); the earlier years are aggregated here from NAPS's raw hourly files on the
same 1-hour basis (a monthly mean needs ≥50% of the month's hours; an annual mean
needs ≥6 months spanning all four quarters). The two eras join smoothly — Toronto
SO₂, for instance, reads ~2.5 ppb in both 2004 and 2005. Smaller communities are
omitted to keep the selector manageable; recent validated data lags the calendar
year by ~12–18 months.

**Cities vs. the national picture.** These city averages complement the
population-weighted *national* indicator on the [Air Quality](air-quality.qmd)
page (which is the standard reference but only national and from 2009). Here the
trade-off is reversed: city-level and back to the 1970s, but a simple station average
rather than a population-weighted exposure estimate. All data are published under
the Open Government Licence – Canada.
:::
